‘“ Spirit of the Nation,” and it sold like wild-fire. 1t received yy.
stmted praise from all parties, even from men like John W ilson Croker
and Lord Macaulay; and the ** Times” pronounced the ver 865 (whick
were all unsigned) as far more dangerous than O’Connell’s speeches,
Soon after, a second part was added, and in October, 1843, the litt]e
volume was taken over for publication by Mr. James Duff Y, “‘to whom,” .
as Gavan Duffy writes, ¢ it was the beginning of great prosperity.” Ip
this edition, issued from 23, Anglesea Street, Dublin, the verses were
signed, but only by an initial or a nom de plume. A new and revised
edition of the ‘‘Spirit of the Nation ’’ was commenced by James Duffy

- on July 1st, 1844, and the music was printed to many of the songs.
It came out as a qu._lrto, in monthly parts, and Davis—who supplied § En
2 characteristic Preface—took considerable trouble over the adaptation 1§ .-

number appeared on December 24th, 1844, and the completed volume 2

was issued early in 1845. Davis was very proud of the get-up of the ¥ H.
wqu, as 1s evident ‘from the following passage in a letter to Jobn | D.
Edward; Pigot: “The music is very well done, and the paper and letter- § the

- : | press are superb. \otlnng like them have been attempted here before.” E litd
f s - This quarto had a wonderful vogue, and a second edition—revised and ? iss
E ' o o enlargt}d—appea.red in August, 1845. In the or1gmwl Preface,  dated E;‘%fmf
2 ~ January lst, 1845, it is stated that there are ‘‘ seventeen original airs” § Ay
- | ot | - and “ twenty-two old Irish airs.”” The final proof sheets of the second , ot

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 180

of the lyrics may not now have the force of former days, but the LTt
‘| marvel is that such a large number has outlived the hurly-burly of =
sixty-seven years. And yet, the reason is not hard to seek: these

Jyrics were inspired by true nationality, by a due sense of national EZ oo
_pride; and quite any army of poets, as if by magic, sprung up to pour -

forth in deathless song the legitimate aspirations of the Irish people.

May these lyrics continue to inspire the young men of Ireland even =
i after the attainment of the goal for which Davis and his fellow-poets '
.gtrove 80 nobly—a goal which seems now all but won—name]y, the - 73 *-
- & making of Ireland ‘‘ a Nation once again.’ =

One of the most successful novels of recent years has been ‘' The
Dop Doctor,”’ whose authorship has hitherto been ‘““ wropt in mistry.”
It now transpires that it is the work of Miss Clo. Graves, who was
born in the barracks of Buttevant, Co. Cork. .

With the present number we conclude tho second vearly volume of
our little venture, and take this opportunity of returning our hearty
thanks to the contributors who have assisted us so ably in the past, ‘‘and
solicit a continuance of their favours,”’ as the enterprising advertisers
say. In the past year, as regards quantity, we have increased our
pages to tha extent of two of our earlier numbers, extra, and as for
- the quaht}',- well—it speaks for itself.  Our aim shall be to make 1%
brlght»er more attractive and useful to our readers every year, end we
feave our friends to do their part by making our existence more
